content: Consider a mass-pulley system as shown in the figure. There is a wedge of mass $M$ and equal wedge angles $\theta$ lying on a rigid horizontal table. The coefficient of friction between the wedge and the table is $\mu$. There are two blocks of mass $m_1$ and $m_2$ lying on the incline of the wedge. The coefficients of friction between the blocks and wedge are $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ as shown in the figure. Consider $m_1>m_2$ and the coefficients of friction ( $\mu, \mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ ) to be less than $\tan \theta$. Gravity is acting downwards with acceleration due to gravity $g$. What should be the value of $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$ so that the system is in equilibrium?
explanation:
$$ \text { The forces acting on the blocks can be described as follows: } $$

$$
\text { For } m_1 \text { : }
$$		 $$
\text { For } m_2 \text { : }
$$
$$
\text { Normal Force: } N_1=m_1 g \cos \theta
$$		 $$
\text { Normal Force: } N_2=m_2 g \cos \theta
$$
$$
\text { Friction Force: } f_1=\mu_1 N_1=\mu_1 m_1 g \cos \theta
$$		 $$
\text { Friction Force: } f_2=\mu_2 N_2=\mu_2 m_2 g \cos \theta
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Component of weight down the incline: }\\
&m_1 g \sin \theta
\end{aligned}
$$
 $$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Component of weight down the incline: }\\
&m_2 g \sin \theta
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Since } m_1 \text { is in equilibrium: }\\
&\begin{gathered}
m_1 g \sin \theta=T+f_1 \\
T=m_1 g\left(\sin \theta-\mu_1 \cos \theta\right)
\end{gathered}
\end{aligned}
$$		 $$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Since } m_2 \text { is in equilibrium: }\\
&\begin{gathered}
m_2 g \sin \theta=T-f_2 \\
T=m_2 g\left(\sin \theta+\mu_2 \cos \theta\right)
\end{gathered}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the two tensions:

$$ m_1 g\left(\sin \theta-\mu_1 \cos \theta\right)=m_2 g\left(\sin \theta+\mu_2 \cos \theta\right) $$

Cancelling out $g$ :

$$ m_1\left(\sin \theta-\mu_1 \cos \theta\right)=m_2\left(\sin \theta+\mu_2 \cos \theta\right) $$

The value of $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$ for the system to be in equilibrium is:

$$ \frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{\mu_2 \cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\sin \theta-\mu_1 \cos \theta} $$
